category_title: Deaf and hard of hearing children with ASD who exhibit escape-motivated tantrums during difficult tasks
student_description: Charlie is an 8-year-old boy with ASD and profound hearing loss who engages in escape-motivated tantrums (screaming, pounding fists, leaving his chair) when faced with difficult reading tasks. He uniquely needs proactive functional communication training (e.g., signing 'break') to appropriately request delays, task interspersal to reduce task aversion, and pre-selected reinforcers to build tolerance for work demands.

criteria.rationale: Charlie needs to be reminded of his appropriate alternative to escape (signing 'break') before he becomes overwhelmed by the work demand.
criteria.check: The tutor reminds Charlie before or at the very beginning of the task that he can appropriately ask for a break or delay if he needs one.

criteria.rationale: Breaking down difficult reading tasks by mixing short, easy requests with harder ones reduces his immediate aversion to the work.
criteria.check: The tutor intersperses easier or shorter tasks (e.g., reading a single sentence) with the more difficult target tasks (e.g., reading a paragraph).

criteria.rationale: Removing the work demand immediately after a tantrum inadvertently reinforces his escape-motivated behavior.
criteria.check: The tutor does NOT remove the work demand or reading material in response to Charlie's avoidance behaviors or tantrums.
